nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated species documented on coral and rocky reef flats, protected shorelines, seaward reefs, and along drop-offs at depths of roughly 0–150 m. Has been reported as a benthic, solitary animal that typically rests with the head protruding from a crevice in the reef structure.

decorPreferences: very large, stable rockwork with caves, crevices, and tunnels large enough to conceal the entire body
rockwork securely anchored to the substrate — the species has been documented rearranging rock and sand
tightly sealed, weighted or mechanically secured lid with no gaps (documented escape artist)
long footprint over depth — at least ~240 cm length is generally advised for adult housing
PVC tubes or artificial caves may be used to supplement live rock if needed for hide structure

diet.notes: A piscivorous predator that has been documented feeding on fishes, cephalopods, and crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts meaty marine foods such as whole fish, silversides, krill, shrimp, squid, and other crustacean flesh. Large meals every 3–4 days tend to be sufficient for adults. Feeding with tongs or a long feeding stick is strongly advised — the species has poor eyesight, relies heavily on smell, and has been known to bite hands by accident. Never hand-feed.

goodWith: very large, robust predatory tankmates not small enough to be swallowed
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed whole (will be eaten)
all crustaceans — shrimp, crabs, lobsters, hermit crabs (natural prey)
other moray eels in all but the largest exhibits
conspecifics (aggression toward similar species has been widely reported)

summary: The Yellow-Edged Moray (Gymnothorax flavimarginatus) is a large Indo-Pacific reef eel reaching roughly 2–2.4 m, distinguished by yellow margins on the dorsal and anal fins. Its adult size, predatory diet, and strong escape tendencies make it suitable only for very large, specialist aquaria.
careNotes:
The Yellow-Edged Moray is widely rated by retailers as 'easy' day-to-day care, but is most accurately considered an expert-level species because of its adult size, escape risk, and predatory diet. The animal itself tends to be hardy on water quality, but housing the full-grown 2–2.4 m eel responsibly is well outside the scope of typical home aquaria.

A tightly sealed, weighted or mechanically secured lid is generally considered essential — the species has been widely documented as an escape artist capable of lifting unweighted covers. Robust biological and mechanical filtration plus a strong protein skimmer are commonly advised given the heavy bioload and large feedings. Rockwork should be secured against collapse: the eel has been reported to rearrange rocks and sand as it moves through the tank.

Reef compatibility is generally listed as 'with caution.' In practice the species will consume any crustacean and any fish small enough to swallow, and its bulk can dislodge unsecured rockwork or corals. Most sources rate it not reef-safe in the conventional sense.

Tank-size cross-source disagreement was wide. LiveAquaria lists 300 gallons (~1136 L); ReefApp recommends 1500 L; Reeflings lists 400 L (a value that appears to describe juvenile housing rather than an adult animal that reaches 2.4 m). Given the confirmed adult size, active swimming behavior, and territorial nature, 1500 L was chosen as the cautious minimum. Because the source spread exceeds 2×, dataStatus is set to needs_review so a human reviewer can sanity-check.

Adult-size cross-source agreement was strong: FishBase 240 cm TL, LiveAquaria 96 in (~244 cm), ReefApp up to 240 cm, Reeflings up to 240 cm. The recorded 180–240 cm range reflects the typical–to–maximum adult length.

Temperature sources were broadly consistent: LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22–26°C), FishBase wild data 23.7–29°C with a mean of 27.8°C, ReefApp ~26°C, Reeflings 22–28°C. Averaged usable range 23–27°C.

Care-level disagreement was substantial: LiveAquaria rates the species 'easy'; this profile rates it 'expert' because the size, escape risk, and predatory diet justify the upgrade despite the animal's biological hardiness.

Ciguatera poisoning has been documented in human consumption of this species in the wild, but this is not relevant to aquarium husbandry. No lifespan data was located across the sources consulted.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: The tank-size disagreement was re-examined against biology. The adult body reaches 240 cm; a tank footprint must accommodate that length, placing the practical minimum around 240 cm × 60 cm × 60 cm ≈ 864 L even before behavioural margin. LiveAquaria's 300-gal (~1136 L) figure is close to this biological floor and almost certainly describes a sub-adult or minimum-viable housing rather than a comfortable adult enclosure. ReefApp's 1500 L is the most conservative and biologically justified recommendation for a fully-grown specimen and was confirmed across that source in a fresh fetch (2026-06-03). Reeflings' 400 L (110 gal) is inconsistent with a 2.4 m animal and is interpreted as juvenile staging. Wikipedia and FishBase carry no aquarium tank-size data. The 1500 L minimum is retained as the responsible adult figure. No source contradiction strong enough to displace it was found; the concern is resolved.
breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding in the sources reviewed. Reproduction in the wild is by external fertilization; aquarium reproduction is generally considered not achieved under home or even public-aquarium conditions for this species.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page: care level easy, temperament semi-aggressive, reef compatible 'with caution', 300 gal minimum, 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, max size 96 in. Most active at night; tight lid required; never hand-feed.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, distribution, adult size, and depth/temperature range. Family Muraenidae, order Anguilliformes; max length 240 cm TL; depth 0–150 m; wild temperature 23.7–29°C (mean 27.8°C); Indo-Pacific from Red Sea/East Africa to French Polynesia, north to Hawaii and Ryukyus, south to New Caledonia, plus Eastern Pacific (Costa Rica, Panama, Galápagos); feeds on cephalopods, fishes, crustaceans; IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 1500 L minimum (largest recommendation), ~26°C, SG ~1.026, adult size up to 240 cm, reef-safe with caution, aggressive toward conspecifics, large meal every 3–4 days, escape specialist, rearranges rocks/sand.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 400 L minimum (low outlier — appears to describe juvenile housing), 22–28°C, pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, adult size up to 240 cm, territorial/best kept alone, not reef-safe, prefers live/frozen foods. Adult-size and water-parameter agreement strong; tank-size figure was discarded as juvenile-only.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented in reviewed sources.
